title: Logarithms
blurb: The inverse of the exponential: log laws, solving exponential and log equations, and the scales that measure earthquakes and sound.

lessons.title: Meet Logarithms
lessons.concreteComparison: You know $2^5 = 32$ forwards: 'two to the fifth is what?' A **logarithm** asks it backwards: '$2$ to WHAT power is $32$?' — written $\log_2 32 = 5$. Every exponential fact you own is secretly two facts, and the log is the one that answers the question banks, biologists, and seismologists actually ask: how long, how many doublings, how strong?
lessons.objective: Read and write logarithms as 'what exponent?' questions, converting fluently between $b^y = x$ and $\log_b x = y$.
concept: **Definition**: $\log_b x = y$ means exactly $b^y = x$. The log is the EXPONENT: $\log_2 32 = 5$ because $2^5 = 32$.
Read $\log_2 32$ aloud as a question: '2 to what power gives 32?'. Every log evaluation is an exponent hunt.
The two forms are the same fact wearing different clothes: $3^4 = 81 \iff \log_3 81 = 4$. Converting between them is this lesson's whole muscle.

House rules (inherited from exponentials): the base $b$ is positive and $\neq 1$, and you can only take logs of POSITIVE numbers — $b^y$ never outputs zero or negatives, so the question '$b$ to what power is $-8$?' has no answer.
lessons.keyIdea: log_b x = y ⟺ b^y = x — the log IS the exponent. Only positive inputs have logs.

facts.title: The question
facts.explanation: Every log is an exponent hunt.

workedExamples.statement: Convert $5^3 = 125$ to log form, and $\log_4 64 = 3$ to exponential form.

commonMistakes.correction: It's neither — it's an EXPONENT: the power that lifts 2 to 32. $\log_2 32 = 5$, not 16 or 64.

commonMistakes.text: Taking the log of a negative number or zero.
commonMistakes.correction: $\log_2(-8)$ asks '2 to what power is $-8$?' — no real power does that ($2^y > 0$ always). Log inputs must be positive.

steps.title: Exponents in reverse
beats: Forwards: $2^5 = ?$ → 32. You've done this for years.

steps.title: Exponential, meet your inverse
steps.teach: Here's $y = 2^x$ from the Grade 10 unit. The exponential answers 'given the exponent, what's the value?' — the LOG reads the same curve the other way: 'given the value, what was the exponent?'. Find 8 on the y-axis, slide to the curve, drop down: $x = 3$. That's $\log_2 8 = 3$, read off the graph.

steps.title: Ask the question
steps.explanation: '4 to what power is 64?' — $4^3 = 64$, so 3. ($16$ is $64÷4$: the division trap.)

steps.title: Convert both ways
beats: Log form: the result moves in, the exponent moves out.
Stuck on a log? Put the fact back in exponential clothes.

steps.title: The exponent hunt
steps.intro: Rewrite in exponential clothes and hunt.

steps.title: Backwards fluency
steps.intro: The log is the exponent — hunt it.

problems.explanation: $2^y$ is never negative. ($\log_2 \tfrac12 = -1$ and $\log_2 1 = 0$ are fine — negative and zero OUTPUTS are legal; negative INPUTS are not.)

steps.title: The tables that steered ships
steps.body: Before calculators, every navigator and astronomer owned a book of log tables — 400 pages of pre-computed exponent-hunts. John Napier published the first in 1614, and Kepler said it 'doubled the life of an astronomer' by halving the arithmetic. Logs were humanity's calculator for 350 years; the slide rule was just logs carved into wood.

steps.title: What you learned
points: $\log_b x = y \iff b^y = x$ — the log is the exponent.
Evaluate by asking 'base to what power gives this?'.
Inputs must be positive; the base is positive and ≠ 1.

lessons.title: Evaluating Logarithms
lessons.concreteComparison: A cashier doesn't recompute $7 \times 8$ — they know it. Logs have the same times-table moments: $\log_b 1 = 0$ always, $\log_b b = 1$ always, and fractional inputs go NEGATIVE ($\log_2 \tfrac18 = -3$). Load the special values once and most log questions become instant reads.
lessons.objective: Evaluate logs instantly at the special values (1, the base, powers and reciprocal powers of the base) including negative and fractional answers.
concept: Two always-true anchors: $\log_b 1 = 0$ (since $b^0 = 1$) and $\log_b b = 1$ (since $b^1 = b$). Any base, every time.
Reciprocals go negative: $\log_2 \tfrac18$ asks '2 to what power is $\tfrac18$?' — $2^{-3} = \tfrac18$, so $-3$. Inputs between 0 and 1 have negative logs.
Roots go fractional: $\log_4 2 = \tfrac12$ because $4^{1/2} = 2$; $\log_8 2 = \tfrac13$. Grade 10's rational exponents, cashing in.
The **common log** $\log x$ (no base shown) means base 10 — and on powers of ten it just counts zeros: $\log 1000 = 3$, $\log 0.01 = -2$.
lessons.keyIdea: Anchors: log_b 1 = 0, log_b b = 1; reciprocals negative, roots fractional; bare log = base 10.

facts.title: Common log
facts.explanation: On powers of 10 it counts zeros.

commonMistakes.correction: $b^0 = 1$ for EVERY base, so $\log_b 1 = 0$. The '1 input' always answers zero — it's the exponential's starting line.
Positive fractions are fine — their logs are just NEGATIVE: $\log_2 \tfrac18 = -3$. Only zero and negatives are out of bounds.

steps.title: Powers of the base
beats: Two anchors, memorized once, used forever.

steps.title: Fractions go negative
beats: $\log_2 \tfrac18$: '2 to what power is an eighth?'
The ladder extends below zero — same rungs, minus signs.

steps.title: Roots go fractional
beats: Rational exponents from Grade 10, paying rent.

steps.title: The common log
steps.explanation: Bare log = base 10, and $10^6$ = a million: the common log counts zeros.

steps.title: Your brain runs on log scales
steps.body: Psychophysics' Weber–Fechner law: human senses perceive RATIOS, not differences. Doubling loudness feels like one equal 'step' whether it's whisper→talk or talk→shout — your ear is taking a logarithm. Same for brightness, pitch, even how children first estimate numbers. Evaluating logs feels natural because your nervous system's been doing it all along.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Inputs in $(0,1)$: negative logs; root inputs: fractional logs.
Bare $\log$ = base 10 — it counts zeros on powers of ten.

lessons.title: The Log Laws
lessons.concreteComparison: Exponents had laws: multiply powers, ADD exponents. Since logs ARE exponents, every exponent law casts a log shadow: $\log(ab) = \log a + \log b$ — multiplication downstairs becomes addition upstairs. Three laws, all inherited, all doing the same job: turning hard operations into easier ones.
lessons.objective: Apply the product, quotient, and power laws to expand, condense, and evaluate logarithmic expressions.
concept: **Product law**: $\log_b(xy) = \log_b x + \log_b y$. Shadow of $b^m \cdot b^n = b^{m+n}$. Check: $\log_2(8 \cdot 4) = \log_2 32 = 5 = 3 + 2$ ✓.
**Quotient law**: $\log_b \tfrac{x}{y} = \log_b x - \log_b y$. Shadow of $b^m / b^n = b^{m-n}$.
**Power law** (the workhorse): $\log_b(x^n) = n \log_b x$ — exponents INSIDE a log walk out front as multipliers. Shadow of $(b^m)^n = b^{mn}$.

Two directions of use: **expand** ($\log_2 \tfrac{x^3 y}{z} = 3\log_2 x + \log_2 y - \log_2 z$) and **condense** ($2\log 5 + \log 4 = \log 100 = 2$). Condensing often makes ugly sums suddenly evaluable.
lessons.keyIdea: Logs turn × into +, ÷ into −, and powers into multipliers — the exponent laws, seen from below.

facts.title: The three laws
facts.latex: \log xy = \log x + \log y \quad \log \tfrac{x}{y} = \log x - \log y \quad \log x^n = n\log x

facts.title: The warning
facts.explanation: No law splits a SUM inside a log.

commonMistakes.correction: The product law splits PRODUCTS only. Numbers kill it: $\log_2(8+8) = 4$, but $\log_2 8 + \log_2 8 = 6$. No law touches $\log(x+y)$.
$\log_2(8^2) = \log_2 64 = 6 = 2\log_2 8$ — the power multiplies. $(\log_2 8)^2 = 9$: different number, different meaning.

steps.title: Shadows of the exponent laws
beats: Logs ARE exponents, so downstairs multiplication…
All three laws arrive this way. Nothing new to prove.

steps.title: No law for sums
beats: Products split. Sums sit tight. Always.

steps: One memorized log unlocks a family.

steps.title: Law enforcement
steps.intro: ×→+, ÷→−, powers out front; sums never split.

problems.explanation: Only the power law. Test the others at $x = 4$: $\log_2 8 = 3 \ne 2+2$; $(\log_2 4)^3 = 8 \ne 6$.

steps.title: The law that shortened WWII
steps.body: Engineers' slide rules multiplied by ADDING lengths — physical rulers marked in logs, exploiting the product law. Every aircraft, bridge, and rocket before 1970 was designed on one; the Apollo engineers carried them to the Moon as calculator backups. $\log(xy) = \log x + \log y$ isn't just algebra — it was the 20th century's multiplication hardware.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Product: ×→+. Quotient: ÷→−. Power: exponents walk out front.
Condense to evaluate; expand to simplify.
$\log(x+y)$ has no law — sums never split.

lessons.title: Solving Exponential Equations
lessons.concreteComparison: Grade 10 left a question dangling: money doubles at $A = 100 \cdot 2^t$ — WHEN does it hit 3200? You could step year by year… or free the trapped exponent in one move. Logs are the key made for this lock: $2^t = 32 \to t = \log_2 32 = 5$. The variable-in-the-exponent problem, finally solved by machine instead of by guessing.
lessons.objective: Solve exponential equations by matching bases or by taking logs, isolating the exponential first.
concept: **Same-base method**: if both sides can wear one base, exponents must match. $2^{x+1} = 32 = 2^5 \to x + 1 = 5 \to x = 4$.
**Log method** (works always): rewrite $b^x = c$ as $x = \log_b c$. $2^x = 32 \to x = \log_2 32 = 5$ — the definition doing the solving.
**Isolate first**: in $5 \cdot 2^x = 40$, divide by 5 BEFORE logging: $2^x = 8 \to x = 3$. The exponential must be alone, like the radical in Grade 10's radical equations.
When the answer isn't whole, it's still exact: $2^x = 10 \to x = \log_2 10$ (a hair above 3, since $2^3 = 8 < 10 < 16$). Bracketing between whole exponents sanity-checks any log answer.
lessons.keyIdea: Match bases if you can; otherwise isolate b^x and apply x = log_b c. Bracket the answer between whole powers.

facts.title: The key
facts.explanation: The definition, run as a solver.

commonMistakes.text: Logging before isolating: taking logs of $5 \cdot 2^x = 40$ term-by-term.
commonMistakes.correction: Divide by 5 FIRST: $2^x = 8$. (Logging the product works with laws — $\log 5 + x\log 2 = \log 40$ — but isolating first is two steps shorter and slip-proof.)
$2^x = 3^x$ doesn't give 'x = x, always true' — for $x \neq 0$ the sides differ. Same-base shortcuts need the SAME base; otherwise, logs.

steps.title: When does it hit 3200?
beats: Grade 10 could only guess-and-check. You have the key.

steps.title: Match the bases
beats: One base on both sides → exponents equal:
Fastest when the match exists. Hunt powers first.

steps.title: The log key
beats: …so $x$ is a bit above 3. Exact answer + sanity range.

steps.title: Clear the multiplier
steps.explanation: Divide by 3: $4^x = 16 \to x = 2$. The multiplier never rides into the log.

steps.title: Key work
steps.intro: Match bases, or isolate and log.

examples.prompt: A dose decays: $D = 400 \cdot (\tfrac12)^t$. When is 25 mg left?

problems.explanation: $5^2 = 25 < 100 < 125 = 5^3$. Bracketing beats blind trust in any calculator.

steps.title: Carbon dating is one log solve
steps.body: A fossil holds 12.5% of its original carbon-14. How old? Solve $(\tfrac12)^{t/5730} = 0.125 = (\tfrac12)^3$ — three half-lives, so $t = 17{,}190$ years. Every radiocarbon date in every museum placard is exactly this lesson's equation, solved the same-base way.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Match bases when possible: $b^m = b^n \Rightarrow m = n$.
Bracket log answers between whole powers as a sanity check.

lessons.title: Solving Log Equations
lessons.concreteComparison: Radical equations taught you the drill: isolate, un-do, CHECK for fakes. Log equations run the same drill with a new un-do: rewrite $\log_2 x = 4$ in exponential clothes ($x = 16$) and you're done — almost. Logs refuse negative inputs, so condensed equations can mint candidates the original never accepts. The check is back, and it has teeth.
lessons.objective: Solve logarithmic equations by converting to exponential form (or equating insides), rejecting candidates outside the domain.
concept: **Single log**: isolate it, then swap costumes. $\log_3(x+2) = 2 \to x + 2 = 3^2 \to x = 7$. Check: $\log_3 9 = 2$ ✓.
**Log = log**: same base on both sides → insides equal. $\log_5(2x-1) = \log_5(x+3) \to 2x - 1 = x + 3 \to x = 4$.
**The check bites**: $x = -2$ makes $\log_2(-2)$ — illegal input. Reject it: only $x = 4$ survives. Every candidate must keep every original log's inside POSITIVE.
lessons.keyIdea: Isolate → exponential clothes (or equate insides). Then check: every log's inside must stay positive — fakes get rejected.

facts.title: The bite
facts.explanation: Logs never accept zero or negatives.

workedExamples.solution: Insides equal: $2x - 1 = x + 3 \to x = 4$. Check: both insides $= 7 > 0$ ✓.
Condense: $x(x-2) = 2^3 = 8 \to x^2-2x-8 = 0 \to x = 4$ or $-2$. Reject $-2$ (negative inside). Answer: $x = 4$.

commonMistakes.text: Skipping the domain check after condensing.
commonMistakes.correction: The algebra happily produces $x = -2$ — and $\log_2(-2)$ is meaningless. Both roots solve the CONDENSED equation; only the positive-inside one solves the original.
The BASE stays the base: $x + 2 = 3^2 = 9$. Swapping base and answer is the classic costume malfunction.

steps.title: Isolate, un-do, check
beats: Radical equations: isolate the root, square, CHECK.
Log equations: isolate the log, exponentiate, CHECK.
Same drill, new un-do. The check returns too…

steps.title: Costume-change solve
steps.explanation: $x + 1 = 4^2 = 16 \to x = 15$. (Base 4 stays the base — $2^4$ is the malfunction.)

steps.title: Strip the wrappers
beats: Same base, both sides wrapped once:

steps.title: Condense, solve, REJECT
beats: Reject. Only $x = 4$. The check has teeth here.

steps.title: Audit the candidates
steps.prompt: Solving $\log_3 x + \log_3(x-6) = 3$ yields $x = 9$ and $x = -3$. The solution set is…
steps.explanation: $x = -3$ puts negatives inside both logs — rejected. $x = 9$: insides 9 and 3, both positive ✓ (and $9 \cdot 3 = 27 = 3^3$ ✓).

steps.title: Drill with teeth
steps.intro: Convert or condense; audit every candidate.

steps.title: Teethy checks
steps.intro: Positive insides or it never happened.

problems.explanation: $x^2 = 16$: both $\pm4$ keep the inside ($16$) positive — BOTH survive! Checks can pass fakes through only when insides go bad; here neither does.

problems.prompt: A candidate makes one log's inside equal $0$. It is…
options: rejected — log of zero is undefined
accepted if other logs are fine
problems.explanation: '$b$ to what power is 0?' has no answer — insides must be strictly positive.

steps.title: The equation that sized the internet
steps.body: In 1998, Google's founders ranked pages with an algorithm whose convergence math runs on logs — and web-scale numbers only fit in human heads logarithmically ('the index grew from $10^7$ to $10^{10}$ pages'). Log equations aren't just solvable; they're how engineers even TALK about systems that grow by powers.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Single log: isolate, then exponential clothes.
Log = log (same base): equate the insides.
Condense → solve → REJECT any candidate with a non-positive inside.

lessons.title: Log Scales
lessons.concreteComparison: A magnitude-7 earthquake isn't 'a bit worse' than a 5 — it shakes $10^2 = 100$ times harder. Richter, decibels, and pH are **log scales**: each step UP multiplies the real quantity by a fixed factor. They exist because nature's ranges are absurd — from rustling leaves to jet engines is a trillion-fold — and only logs fold a trillion into a friendly 0-to-120.
lessons.objective: Read and compare log-scale measurements (Richter, decibels, pH), converting scale differences into real-world ratios.
concept: **The rule**: on a log scale, DIFFERENCES in the reading are RATIOS in reality. Richter: each whole step = ×10 ground motion, so mag 7 vs mag 5 is $10^{7-5} = 100×$.
**Decibels**: sound intensity, 10 dB = ×10. A 90 dB concert vs a 60 dB conversation: $10^{(90-60)/10} = 10^3 = 1000×$ the intensity.
**pH**: acidity, each step DOWN = ×10 more acidic. pH 3 soda vs pH 7 water: $10^{7-3} = 10^4 = 10{,}000×$ more acidic.

Why log scales exist: the raw ranges span factors of $10^{12}$ or more. A linear dial would need a kilometer of tickmarks; a log dial folds each ×10 into one step. Reading them is just this unit's math: differences upstairs, ratios downstairs.
lessons.keyIdea: Log scales turn ratios into differences: each step = one ×factor. Difference of d steps = 10^d in reality (Richter, pH; dB use steps of 10).

workedExamples.statement: How much harder does a magnitude 7 quake shake than a magnitude 5?
A concert is 90 dB; a conversation 60 dB. Intensity ratio?
Soda has pH 3, water pH 7. How many times more acidic is the soda?

commonMistakes.text: Reading scale differences as real differences: 'mag 7 is 2 units worse than mag 5'.
commonMistakes.correction: Two STEPS = a factor of $10^2 = 100$. Log scales hide multiplication inside subtraction — that's their whole job.

commonMistakes.text: Doubling the reading to double the quantity: '6 dB is twice 3 dB'.
commonMistakes.correction: Each +10 dB multiplies intensity ×10; doubling a READING can mean a millionfold change. Never do linear arithmetic on a log dial.

tryIt.statement: pH 2 lemon juice vs pH 6 milk: acidity ratio?

steps.title: Why log scales exist
beats: Rustling leaves → jet engine: a TRILLION-fold in energy.
A linear dial would need a kilometer of tickmarks.
The fix: each dial step = one ×10. Fold the trillion into 12.
Richter, decibels, pH — all the same fold.

steps.title: Differences are ratios
beats: the log laws, running civilization's dials.

steps.title: Quake arithmetic
steps.prompt: A magnitude 6 quake vs a magnitude 3: the ground moves…

steps.title: Tens of a bel
beats: Headphones at 100 dB vs 70 dB: also $1000×$. Ears, beware.
(+3 dB ≈ ×2 — the sound engineer's favorite shortcut.)

steps.title: Acid arithmetic
steps.prompt: Vinegar (pH 2) vs baking-soda water (pH 9): the vinegar is…
steps.explanation: Seven steps of ×10: ten million times. pH compresses chemistry's wildest ratios into 0–14.

examples.prompt: The 1960 Chile quake (mag ≈ 9.5) vs a mag 6.5 quake: motion ratio?
City traffic: 70 dB. A rock concert: 110 dB. Intensity ratio?
How many mag-5 quakes' worth of shaking equals one mag 7?
steps: Each mag-5 is $10^{-2}$ of a mag-7's motion.

steps.title: Ratio fluency
steps.intro: Steps to powers; never linear arithmetic.

problems.prompt: 40 dB library vs 80 dB vacuum cleaner: intensity ratio…
problems.explanation: 4 tens of dB → $10^4 = 10{,}000×$. ('Twice the dB' ≠ twice the sound!)

problems.prompt: A solution 1000× more acidic than pH 8 has pH…
problems.explanation: ×1000 = 3 steps MORE acidic = 3 steps DOWN: pH 5.

problems.prompt: Every whole step on a log-10 scale multiplies the quantity by…
problems.explanation: That's the definition — and why a 12-step dial spans a trillion.

steps.title: Your playlist is logarithmic too
steps.body: Musical octaves are a log scale of frequency: every octave UP doubles the Hz. A piano's 88 keys span $27.5$ Hz to $4186$ Hz — a $152×$ range folded into 7-and-a-bit equal-feeling octaves, because your ear hears RATIOS. Equal-tempered tuning divides each ×2 into twelve equal log-steps: every semitone is $2^{1/12}$. Music theory is applied logarithms.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Log scales: reading differences = real ratios ($10^d$).
Richter and pH step by ×10; decibels by ×10 per 10 dB.
They exist to fold trillion-fold ranges into friendly dials.

practice.statement: Convert $10^4 = 10000$ to log form; convert $\log_2 x = 5$ to exponential form and solve.
Magnitude 6.9 vs 4.9: how much stronger is the shaking?

testYourself.statement: A whisper is 30 dB; a chainsaw 110 dB. Intensity ratio?
